::: {.proof}

Adapted from [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 2, p. 258];
the original profile-likelihood argument is due to @johansen1983extension.

Assume, as in the partial-likelihood proof, that there are no tied event times,
so each ordered event time $t_i$ corresponds to exactly one event.
Let $K$ denote the number of distinct event times $t_1 < \cdots < t_K$.
As in the partial-likelihood proof, we color the
$\blue{\text{baseline-hazard pieces}}$ — here the point masses
$\blue{h_{0i}}$ that we profile out — and the
$\red{\text{focal hazard-ratio pieces}}$, so the eye can follow how each
piece moves through the algebra.

The full censored-data likelihood for the proportional-hazards model is

$$
\Lik\sb{\vb,\,\haz_0(\cdot)}
= \prod_{j = 1}^{n}
    \haz(\tilde{T}_j \mid \vx_j)^{\delta_j}\,
    \surv(\tilde{T}_j \mid \vx_j),
$$

where $\delta_j$ is the event indicator and $\tilde{T}_j$ the observed time
for subject $j$ — the same quantities denoted $D_j$ and $Y_j$ in the [censored-data likelihood](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#lem-censored-lik) lemma,
so $\tilde{T}_j = Y_j = \min(T_j, C_j)$ and $\delta_j = D_j$.
Substituting
$\haz(t \mid \vx) = \blue{\haz_0(t)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx)}$ (by @thm-ph-haz-decomp)
and
$\surv(t \mid \vx) = \expf{-\blue{\cuhaz_0(t)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx)}}$
(by @thm-ph-cuhaz, using $\surv(t) = \expf{-\cuhaz(t)}$ from the [survival/cumulative hazard relationship](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#cor-surv-int-haz))
gives

$$
\Lik\sb{\vb,\,\haz_0(\cdot)}
= \prod_{j = 1}^{n}
    \sb{\blue{\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}}^{\delta_j}\,
    \expf{-\blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}}.
$$

Fix $\vb$ and maximize over $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
Subjects with $\delta_j = 0$ contribute only the survival term;
for such subjects, $\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)^{\delta_j} = 1$ regardless of $\haz_0$.
Adding mass to $\haz_0$ at a non-event time $t$ increases $\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)$
for every subject with $\tilde{T}_j \ge t$, penalizing their survival terms,
without any compensating gain in a hazard-density factor.
Conversely, for each event subject $j$ with $\delta_j = 1$, treat
$\haz_0$ as a discrete hazard measure: $\haz_0(t_i)$ equals the
point-mass weight placed at $t_i$. Consider any allocation of a
fixed total mass $h_{0i}$ to a neighborhood of $t_i$:
$\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)$ depends only on that total (the survival
penalty $\expf{-\cuhaz_0(\tilde T_j)\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$ is therefore
unchanged), but the hazard-density factor
$\haz_0(t_i)^{\delta_j}$ is maximized when all of that mass is
concentrated as a single point at $t_i$ (so $\haz_0(t_i) = h_{0i}$);
spreading the same total across a wider interval would reduce the
per-point weight at $t_i$, lowering $\haz_0(t_i)$ below $h_{0i}$,
while leaving the survival penalty fixed.
The likelihood is therefore maximized by a hazard
that places point masses only at the observed event times:

$$
\blue{\haz_0(t)} = \begin{cases}
\blue{h_{0i}}, & t = t_i \text{ for some } i \in \set{1, \dots, K},\\
0,      & \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
$$

with $\blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)} = \sum_{i\ :\ t_i \le \tilde{T}_j} \blue{h_{0i}}$.
Expanding the survival exponent and swapping the order of summation:

$$
\ba
\sum_j \blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}
&= \sum_j \red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)} \sum_{i:\ t_i \le \tilde{T}_j} \blue{h_{0i}}
&& \text{(expand } \blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)} \text{ as point masses)}
\\
&= \sum_i \blue{h_{0i}} \sum_{j:\ \tilde{T}_j \ge t_i} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}
&& \text{(swap the order of summation)}
\\
&= \sum_i \blue{h_{0i}} \sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)},
&& \text{(}R(t_i) = \{j : \tilde{T}_j \ge t_i\}\text{)}
\ea
$$

Here $R(t_i)$ uses the "at risk *at* $t_i$" convention
(the [risk set definition](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-risk-set))
with the $\ge$ boundary — a subject censored exactly at $t_i$ is in
$R(t_i)$ and contributes $\blue{h_{0i}}$ to $\blue{\cuhaz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}$.
Collecting the inner sum into the **risk-set score total**
$\red{S_i} = \sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$
— the same piece defined in the partial-likelihood proof
(@eq-ph-risk-set-score) — the survival exponent becomes
$\sum_i \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}$.
Reindexing the hazard-density product: subjects with $\delta_j = 0$
contribute a factor of $\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)^0 = 1$ (no contribution),
and for each subject with $\delta_j = 1$ their event occurred at some
$t_i$, so $\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j) = h_{0i}$:

$$
\ba
\prod_{j=1}^n \sb{\blue{\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}}^{\delta_j}
&= \prod_{j:\,\delta_j=1} \blue{\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}
   && \text{(terms with } \delta_j = 0 \text{ equal } 1\text{, dropped)}
\\
&= \prod_{i=1}^K \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}.
   && \text{(}\blue{\haz_0(\tilde{T}_j)} = \blue{h_{0i}} \text{ for event } j \text{ at event time } t_i\text{)}
\ea
$$

Factoring $\expf{-\sum_i \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}} = \prod_i \expf{-\blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}}$
and combining factor-by-factor with the hazard-density product:

$$
\ba
\Lik\sb{\vb,\,h_{01},\dots,h_{0K}}
&= \underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}_{\text{hazard-density}}
   \cdot \underbrace{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \expf{-\blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}}}_{\text{survival}}
\\
&= \prod_{i = 1}^{K}
    \blue{h_{0i}}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}\;
    \expf{-\blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}}.
\ea
$$

The log-likelihood is separable in the $\blue{h_{0i}}$ (each summand $\log \blue{h_{0i}} - \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}$ involves only $\blue{h_{0i}}$):

$$
\loglik\sb{\vb,\,h_{01},\dots,h_{0K}}
= \sum_{i = 1}^{K} \log\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}
  + \sum_{i = 1}^{K}
    \cb{\log \blue{h_{0i}} - \blue{h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}}.
$$

Differentiating with respect to $h_{0i}$ and setting the derivative to zero:

$$
\ba
\frac{1}{\blue{h_{0i}}} - \red{S_i} &= 0
&& \text{(stationarity condition)}
\\
\Longrightarrow\quad \blue{\hat h_{0i}} &= \frac{1}{\red{S_i}}.
&& \text{(solve for } \blue{\hat h_{0i}} \text{)}
\ea
$$

The second derivative $-1/\blue{h_{0i}}^2 < 0$ confirms this critical point is a maximum.

Summing over event times $t_i \le t$ gives the **Breslow estimator**
of the baseline cumulative hazard:

$$
\ba
\blue{\hat \cuhaz_0(t)}
&= \sum_{t_i \le t} \blue{\hat h_{0i}}
&& \text{(sum the estimated point masses up to } t \text{)}
\\
&= \sum_{t_i \le t} \frac{1}{\red{S_i}}.
&& \text{(substitute } \blue{\hat h_{0i}} = 1/\red{S_i} \text{)}
\ea
$$

where $\red{S_i} = \sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$ as before.
With tied event times, the numerator generalizes to $d_i$,
the number of events at $t_i$
(see [@klein2003survival, §8.8] for the tie-handling adjustments).

Substituting $\hat h_{0i}$ back into the profile likelihood:

$$
\ba
\Lik\sb{\vb,\,\hat h_{01},\dots,\hat h_{0K}}
&= \prod_{i = 1}^{K}
    \blue{\hat h_{0i}}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}\;
    \expf{-\blue{\hat h_{0i}}\,\red{S_i}}
\\
&= \prod_{i = 1}^{K}
    \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}\;
    \expf{-\red{S_i} / \red{S_i}}
    && \text{(substituting } \blue{\hat h_{0i}} = 1/\red{S_i}\text{)}
\\
&= \prod_{i = 1}^{K}
    \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}\;
    e^{-1}
    && \text{(since } \red{S_i}/\red{S_i} = 1\text{)}
\\
&= e^{-K}
   \cdot \prod_{i = 1}^{K} \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}.
\ea
$$

Under the no-ties assumption each event time $t_i$ has $d_i = 1$, so
$\prod_{i=1}^K \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}$
equals the partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\vb)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.
Hence

$$\Lik\sb{\vb,\,\hat h_{01},\dots,\hat h_{0K}} = e^{-K}\,\Lik^*(\vb).$$

The factor $e^{-K}$ does not depend on $\vb$,
so the profile likelihood is proportional to the partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\vb)$.
This proportionality justifies treating the partial likelihood as a profile likelihood
for $\vb$, with $\haz_0(\cdot)$ concentrated out.

:::
